coredata.dc:title: Multi-color emission composites of white carbon dots and dyes through inner filter effect
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ren, Hong
Wang, Jinbao
Yu, Shihua
Wu, Ping
coredata.dc:description: A series of multi-color carbon dot composites were obtained by transforming white carbon dots (WCDs) into three primary colors using the dyes' inner filter effect (IFE). WCDs were combined with three distinct dyes (methyl red, bromocresol green, and methylene blue), and three fluorescence colors (red, yellow, and blue) were obtained. The colors of the emitting light excited by the same UV light were similar to the colors of the respective dyes, and have a large Stokes shift. The presence of the IFE between WCDs and dyes was proved through a variety of methods, the analysis of the relationship between the UV of dyes and fluorescence spectra of WCDs, the excitation-emission matrix, as well as the comparison PL decay profiles of WCDs before and after dye addition. The Stern-Volmer quenching constant K SV exhibited no temperature dependence during the IFE process with increasing concentrations of dyes. The UV spectra of the mixture of WCDs and dyes were simply the sum of two individual components, which implies that there is no chemical interaction between WCDs and dyes. Since methyl red and bromocresol green are acid-base indicators, the fluorescence color of the carbon dot composite also changed continuously during the dilute NaOH solution titration process. In furtherment, distributing the binary system of “WCDs + dye” within poly (vinyl alcohol) (PVA), three colors fluorescent composite films of “WCDs / Dye / PVA” were produced successfully.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot composites
Dye
Inner filter effect
Regulation of multiple color emission

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The Inner filter effect in the transition of Carbon Dots from white to colorful fluorescence composites
Achieving the multicolor fluorescence composites through WCDs mixed with dyes
Using pH sensing properties of dyes to obtain CD composites with different fluorescence colors
Preparing WCDs/Dye/PVA composite films with vibrant emissions
Universality and advantage of multi-color emission composites formed by white carbon dots and dyes
The synthesis and characterization of WCDs
General procedure for fluorescence and UV measurement

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a-c) TEM images, Raman spectra and fluorescence emission spectra (Iex = 336 nm) of WCDs in 2.0 mL ethanol solutions with increasing the volume (from 0 to 100 µL) of WCDs. (d-f) UV-Vis absorption spectrum of dyes and fluorescence spectra of WCDs, “WCDs + dyes” binary system. RD for methyl red, YD for bromocresol green, and BD for methylene blue. (g) Excitation–emission matrix for WCDs, (h) WCDs + bromocresol green (2 g/L, 30 µL), (i) WCDs + methylene blue (2 g/L, 30 µL), (j-l) WCDs + methyl red (2 g/L) with increasing volume (30, 50–70 µL).

(a-c) The UV-Vis absorption spectra of dyes, WCDs, and their mixture. (d-f) Stern-Volmer plots for the dyes quenching of WCDs fluorescence at three different temperatures (283 K, 293 K and 303 K). (g-i) PL decay profiles of the emission between WCDs and “WCDs + dyes” binary system. RD for methyl red, YD for bromocresol green, and BD for methylene blue.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra (Iex = 336 nm) of WCDs in solutions with increasing the volume of methyl red (RD, 2 g/L) from 0 to 240 µL. (b) for bromocresol green (YD, 2 g/L) from 0 to 280 µL. (c) for methylene blue (BD, 2 g/L) from 0 to 120 µL. (d) The ratio plot of fluorescence emission intensity at 678 nm to 515 nm (I678/I515), and the plot of I515 as RD volume increases. (e) I580/ I427 for bromocresol green (YD). (f) I450/ I655 for methylene blue (BD). (g-i) Calculated CIE coordinates from fluorescence emission spectra (Iex = 336 nm) of WCDs with increasing dye contents. (g) for methyl red (RD). (h) for bromocresol green (YD). (i) for methylene blue (BD).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of “WCDs + methyl red” binary system with the addition of 0.1 M NaOH in solutions (Iex = 336 nm). (b) Calculated CIE coordinates from fluorescence emission spectra of “WCDs + methyl red” with NaOH titration. (c) Photographs of “WCDs + methyl red” binary system with increasing 0.1 M NaOH volume (0, 2, 4, 8, 12, 16, 20, 30, 40 µL) (Top: natural light; Bottom: under UV lamp). (d) Fluorescence spectra of “WCDs + bromocresol green” binary system with the addition of 0.1 M NaOH in solutions (Iex = 328 nm). (e) Calculated CIE coordinates from fluorescence emission spectra of “WCDs + bromocresol green” with NaOH titration. (f) Photographs of “WCDs + bromocresol green” binary system with increasing 0.1 M NaOH volume (0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14, 16 µL) (Top: natural light; Bottom: under UV lamp).

(a) The “Transmittance %-nm” profile of the red, yellow and blue films. (b) Normalized PL spectra of WCDs with dyes dispersed in PVA composite films under I = 365 nm UV excitation. (c) Photo fluorescence images of CDs/PVA composite films under natural (up) and UV light (bottom) All three films are made using a 15 % PVA solution and WCDs (6.25 µL for the red film, 3.75 µL for the yellow and blue films) with concentrations of methyl red (0.053 g/L), bromocresol green (0.014 g/L), and methylene blue (0.17 g/L).
Strategy of multi-color fluorescent CD composites by combining WCDs with dyes based on inner filter effort (IFE).

head.ce:title: Multi-color emission composites of white carbon dots and dyes through inner filter effect

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Jilin Institute of Chemical Technology, Jilin City 132022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Jilin Institute of Chemical Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Jilin Institute of Chemical Technology, Jilin City 132022, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A series of multi-color carbon dot composites were obtained by transforming white carbon dots (WCDs) into three primary colors using the dyes' inner filter effect (IFE). WCDs were combined with three distinct dyes (methyl red, bromocresol green, and methylene blue), and three fluorescence colors (red, yellow, and blue) were obtained. The colors of the emitting light excited by the same UV light were similar to the colors of the respective dyes, and have a large Stokes shift. The presence of the IFE between WCDs and dyes was proved through a variety of methods, the analysis of the relationship between the UV of dyes and fluorescence spectra of WCDs, the excitation-emission matrix, as well as the comparison PL decay profiles of WCDs before and after dye addition. The Stern-Volmer quenching constant K SV exhibited no temperature dependence during the IFE process with increasing concentrations of dyes. The UV spectra of the mixture of WCDs and dyes were simply the sum of two individual components, which implies that there is no chemical interaction between WCDs and dyes. Since methyl red and bromocresol green are acid-base indicators, the fluorescence color of the carbon dot composite also changed continuously during the dilute NaOH solution titration process. In furtherment, distributing the binary system of “WCDs + dye” within poly (vinyl alcohol) (PVA), three colors fluorescent composite films of “WCDs / Dye / PVA” were produced successfully.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Strategy of multi-color fluorescent Carbon dots (CDs) by combining white Carbon dots (WCDs) with dyes based on inner filter effort (IFE).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Dyes possess a wide range of hues, though they are only discernible under proper illumination. As an object color, the color of a dye results from the reflection of visible light that remains after the dye has absorbed a portion of it, as perceived by the human eye. Unless a dye is fluorescent, it cannot be seen in the dark, and its fluorescence can only be observed at the appropriate excitation wavelength. Apart from sunlight, most other sources of white light are artificial, such as white LED [1], which emit light through semiconductor quantum dots [2], rare earth complexes [3], and so on [4,5]. Meanwhile, carbon dots (CDs) with sizes smaller than 10 nm have garnered immense interest due to their tunable photoluminescence (PL), low toxicity, water solubility, photostability, inexpensive production, and easy surface modification [6,7]. These advantageous features have made CDs useful in various fields, particularly in biosensing, bioimaging, light-emitting diodes, full-color displays, and optoelectronic devices [8–11]. Most of the white light carbon dots (WCDs) reported so far have been created by one-step synthesis or combining multicomponent light emitters with different emission colors [12,13].

The prism experiments by Newton demonstrated that white light, such as that emitted by the sun, is actually comprised of a diverse range of colored light waves. The Grsassmann color mixing law suggests that by creating fluorescent CDs with emission wavelengths close to the three primary colors of light, their fluorescence can be used to synthesize a variety of new colors. This approach not only allows for tunable and controllable fluorescence, but also offers increased functional value for CDs [14–16]. Regulating fluorescent color requires material engineering, aggregation-induced emission, and device fabrication techniques [17]. In a single-material system, in situ chemical tuning (e.g., stimuli response using pH, light, temperature, solvent, and special substance control) is preferred for adjusting fluorescent color [18–21]. However, accurately controlling the chemical structure of CDs remains a challenge in achieving precise material fluorescent color manipulation.

In the realm of synthesized light, a fundamental principle dictates that the absence of a constituent-colored light within a white light blend yields a mixed color of the remaining lights. This mixed color corresponds to the complementary color of the missing light. Such a principle also applies to object colors present in natural sunlight. The removal or filtering out of this type of colored light can be achieved through various techniques, such as photoelectron transfer and inner filter effects (IFE). The IFE is frequently employed in the design of ratiometric fluorescence probes to measure the relative concentrations of two distinct substances. This technique relies on the fact that different fluorescent substances have different absorption and emission spectra, and their fluorescence intensity depends on their relative concentrations. The inner filter effect (IFE) can be employed to selectively filter out the fluorescence of one substance [22–26]. However, there is currently rare research on using this effect to adjust the color of carbon dots [27,28]. Inspired by natural light absorption by dyes, we are exploring the chemical and physical phenomena that regulate fluorescence in WCDs by utilizing light absorption by dyes. Our approach involves classical dyes of different shades, such as methyl red, bromocresol green, and methylene blue, with WCDs to produce a variety of highly fluorescent CDs’ composites with diverse emission colors both in the liquid and solid phase media, such as red, yellow, blue, and more. ( Scheme 1)

ce:section.ce:section-title: The Inner filter effect in the transition of Carbon Dots from white to colorful fluorescence composites
ce:section.ce:para: When it comes to creating white carbon dots (WCDs), there are two commonly used methods. The first is the one-pot method, which usually involves hydrothermal synthesis and solvothermal reaction. The second method refers to mixing carbon dots of different colors to achieve the desired white color. Both methods have successfully produced high-quality white carbon dots for a variety of applications [12,29]. Although the fluorescence intensity of WCDs varies at different wavelengths, they generally cover most of the visible light region. When the different dyes with various spectra are introduced into the system of WCDs, a part of the colored light colors will be absorbed by dyes. Then the spectra of WCDs could be manipulated by different types of dyes. It is as if the CDs illuminate the dye that does not emit light itself, and the luminescent nature of the CDs is regulated by the dye (Scheme 1).

The absorption of light by the dye is a factor in fluorescence quenching phenomena such as the inner filtration effect (IFE). When the absorption wavelength range of the dyes, the quenching agent, covers the excitation or emission light of the fluorescent material, the IFE occurs, and fluorescence of the corresponding wavelength of the fluorescent molecule is quenched [26].

To clarify, the WCDs in this article have been prepared by tartaric acid (Tar) and o-phenylenediamine (oPD) through classical hydrothermal method slightly adjusted according to literature, exhibiting a broad PL ranging from 360 to 800 nm ( Fig. 1a-c) [29]. As shown in the Raman spectra, the common features were observed in the 1200–1800 cm−1 region, where the G band (∼1574 cm−1) originates from graphitic sp2 carbon, the D band (∼1348 cm−1) indicates the existence of disorder structures or defects in the WCDs. The results proved that the graphitic core and surface-related state co-existed in the WCDs. The high-resolution TEM image of WCDs shows well-resolved lattice fringes and uniform nanoparticles with average sizes of ∼5 nm. Fig s1a shows the morphology of WCDs tested using atomic force microscopy (AFM), and the yellow spots represent quantum dots. Analysis shows that WCDs have good dispersibility, with the thickness ranging from 0.246 to 1.395 nm, indicating that they comprise 1–4 layers of graphene (with a single-layer graphene thickness of 0.3 nm). As can be seen from Fig. 1c, at lower concentrations, the fluorescence emission of WCDs exhibits strong emission in the 400–550 nm and 650–800 nm regions. With the increasing concentration of WCDs, the fluorescence emission in the 550–650 nm region is gradually enhanced, so that the WCDs exhibit emission in the entire visible light region, and it can also be seen from the chromaticity diagram that the chromaticity coordinates of the WCDs also tend to the white light region (Fig s1b). To adjust the fluorescence of the WCDs, red, yellow, and blue dyes were selected, which are methyl red, bromocresol green, and methylene blue.

The methyl red has a main absorbance band at 495 nm, which precisely overlaps with the green fluorescence of WCDs, indicating that methyl red can significantly absorb the region of green light (480–550 nm with the center at around 505 nm) via the IFE (Fig. 1d, Fig s2). There is less overlap between the absorption wavelength range of methyl red and the fluorescence of other emission wavelength ranges of the WCDs. Therefore, in the existence of methyl red, the green fluorescence of WCDs decreased obviously. From the change of the spectra, it can be seen that the fluorescence of WCDs is regulated by methyl red to only the emission of red regions. Similar results have been seen in the regulation of the fluorescence of WCDs by two other dyes (bromocresol green and methylene blue). As shown in Fig. 1e, the absorptions for bromocresol green were around 420 nm, and the absorption of purple light (400–450 nm with the center at around 420 nm) is strongest in visible region, and the fluorescence of WCDs is tuned to only the emission of yellow regions. While the absorption band of methylene blue is around 655 nm, which absorbs red light to a large extent (650–750 nm with the center at around 700 nm), and the fluorescence of WCDs is tuned to only the emission of blue regions (Fig. 1f) (Fig s3).

The excitation-emission matrix fluorescence (EEMF) spectroscopy can cope well with broad fluorescence peaks and provide more detailed spectral information (Fig. 1g-l). As shown in Fig. 1g, WCDs have a wide range of fluorescence emission, including the ultraviolet, visible, and near-infrared regions, when the excitation wavelength is in the range of 300–350 nm, and the emission is strongest when the excitation wavelength is around 320 nm. This also indicates that the fluorescence of the complex has a large Stokes shift, especially in the deep red region, where the emission wavelength is more than twice the excitation wavelength. This facilitates the regulation of the fluorescence color of the WCDs, which can be obtained at different wavelengths by a single excitation wavelength near 320 nm. Obviously, with an increased amount (30, 50–70 µL) of methyl red (2 g/L) (Fig. 1g-l), the fluorescence intensity around 495 nm fades away, and the fluorescence peak over 650 nm has emerged as the relatively strong fluorescence intensity in EEMF spectrum of “WCDs + methyl red”. A similar situation can also be observed in “WCDs + bromocresol green” and “WCDs + methylene blue” systems. As for “WCDs + bromocresol green” (Fig. 1h), the fluorescent region around 420 nm has been screened by bromocresol green, the fluorescence intensity around 520 nm has become the strongest emission area in visible light region. For “WCDs + methylene blue” (Fig. 1i), the fluorescent region around 655 nm has been filtered by methylene blue, and the fluorescence intensity around 450 nm can be observed clearly. Thus, absorption of fluorescence by dyes at different wavelengths of WCDs is predominantly responsible for tuning WCDs to multi-color emission.

To further investigate the reasons for the variation in fluorescence spectra, we compared the UV spectra of the WCDs before and after the addition of dye ( Fig. 2a-c). In the visible region, the absorption spectrum of the mixture is the sum of the absorbance (A) of the two individual components of WCDs and dye, that is, the simple superposition of the UV-Vis spectrum of WCDs and dye, and no new absorption peaks are generated. In detail, there are two main peaks around 420 nm and 495 nm of “WCDs + methyl red” that are attributed to the WCDs and dye. For bromocresol green, the central peak is around 420 nm for the overlap of WCDs and dyes. There are two apparent peaks at 420 and 655 nm, corresponding to WCDs and methylene blue.

To examine how dyes quench the fluorescence of WCDs, the quenching constant K SV between WCDs and dyes was determined through fluorescence titration at three different temperatures (283 K, 293 K, and 303 K). The Stern-Volmer plots indicated a linear relation between F0/F and the concentration of dyes, but the slopes were not temperature-dependent. Taking methyl red as an example, we gained the K SV at 283 K, 293 K, and 303 K, which are 1.11 × 106, 0.93 × 106, and 1.00 × 106, respectively. This suggests that the quenching mechanism is most likely IFE (Table s1, Fig. 2d-f).

The quenching efficiency of a fluorophore species can be described using the Stern-Volmer equation:
F0/F = 1 + K
SV [D]

Moreover, the PL decay profiles of WCDs before and after addition of methyl red is gained (Fig. 2g-i). The two curves are similar, showing the PL lifetimes of 2.09 and 2.06 ns. The mechanism of Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET) can also be excluded, for the PL lifetime of the donor remains in IFE, but declines during FRET process. As for bromocresol green and methylene blue (PL lifetimes is 2.20 ns for bromocresol green and 2.21 ns for methylene blue), the situation is as same as the methyl red. Based on the mechanism research results, the interaction between a series of dyes and WCDs can be concluded as the colors of light are regulated through the IFE effort of dye on WCDs.

As a supplement, the excitation spectra of “WCDs + dye” binary system are investigated, indicating the intensity of the excitation spectra have also been weakened to a certain extent after the addition of dyes owing to the inner filtering effect. The weakening of the excitation spectra (λex = 470 nm, 575 nm, and 690 nm) intensity has led to a decrease in the fluorescence intensity of emission spectra (Fig s4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Achieving the multicolor fluorescence composites through WCDs mixed with dyes
ce:section.ce:para: The "WCDs + methyl red" solution, containing varying concentrations of methyl red, changes color when exposed to both natural and ultraviolet light. The solution starts out as white and gradually turns red along with the increment of methyl red concentration. (Fig s5a) Firstly, the spectral control property of methyl red in WCDs solution was examined. As the amount of methyl red increases, the color of the solution of WCDs changes gradually to magenta in daylight. This color change is accompanied by the obvious decrease of the fluorescence intensity around 450–550 nm ( Fig. 3a).

The plot of intensity Imin at 515 nm against methyl red content shows a curved pattern and the intensity drops down to 19.02 % accompanied as the volume of methyl red (2 g/L) increases from 0 to 40 μL. The fluorescence intensity decline tends to be stable when the volume of methyl red reaches 60 µL. After the addition of methyl red volume exceeding 160 µL, the ratio of fluorescence intensity at 678 nm and 515 nm remained stable at ∼77.4 (Fig. 3d). It can be inferred that methyl red has a selective absorption of fluorescence in the green region, whereas the "WCDs + methyl red" system produces the complementary color of red. The CIE color coordinates of "WCDs + methyl red" solutions have been calculated (Table s2), and the latter 11 points exhibit colors from reddish purple, purplish red to red regions. When the volume of methyl red is increased to 240 µL, the CIE coordinate of (0.58, 0.33) corresponds to the typical red color emission, as shown in Fig. 3g. However, as the concentration of methyl red continues to increase, the intensity of red light in the fluorescence spectrum has shown a trend of getting smaller, and the red fluorescence gradually weakens (Fig s5a).

After the addition of bromocresol green and methylene blue to WCDs solution, two series of solutions (one yellow and one blue) were successfully gained. These solutions showed a gradient of fluorescence intensity when exposed to 365 nm ultraviolet light (Fig s5b-c). From the plot of fluorescence intensity against dye content followed a curve pattern, the fluorescence similar to the color of the dye is more prominent, when the volume of bromocresol green (2 g/L) reached around 240 µL and the volume of methylene blue (2 g/L) reached around 60 µL. The addition of bromocresol green resulted in a fluorescence intensity ratio of 129 times between 580 and 427 nm in "WCDs + bromocresol green".

Meanwhile, the addition of methylene blue led to an increase in the fluorescence intensity at 450 nm to 114 times that of 650 nm. The CIE color coordinates of both "WCDs + bromocresol green" and "WCDs + methylene blue" solutions have been calculated and presented in Table s2. The CIE values confirm that spectral color regulation is feasible, with the last point of bromocresol green (0.40, 0.56) and methylene blue (0.16, 0.04) representing typical green and blue colors, respectively.

Initial CIE values of WCDs are (0.22, 0.30), (0.26, 0.30), and (0.25,0.31), the values are relatively close and within the white range. Through the combination of dyes and WCDs, a universal method for producing multicolor CDs that emit bright and stable red, yellow, and blue colors with varying intensity and hue has been established, which was confirmed by the fluorescence spectra and CIE color coordinates value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Using pH sensing properties of dyes to obtain CD composites with different fluorescence colors
ce:section.ce:para: Since the two dyes, methyl red and bromocresol green, are acid-base indicators, to understand better the regulation of WCDs fluorescence by the dye, the effect of the color change of the dyes on the fluorescence of the composite system was investigated, when the pH of the system changed. As expected, adding NaOH to the binary solution of WCDs and methyl red changed the magenta solution to a yellow color ( Fig. 4a-c). This observation is related to the decrease in the fluorescence peak above 650 nm (acid form) and the appearance of a new band at 512 nm (base form). As the volume of NaOH (0.1 M) increased from 0 to 40 µL, the wavelength displayed a blue shift due to the conversion of different types of methyl red and changes in the inner filtration band. The yellow color can be almost completely reversed back to the original magenta color by adding an appropriate amount of HCl aqueous solution (0.1 M).

Similarly, the pH influence on WCDs and bromocresol green was also investigated. Fig. 4 shows a significant fluorescence increase around 450 nm in the visible range when 0.1 M NaOH ranging from 0 to 16 µL is added (Fig. 4d-f). Two series of solutions were adjusted using diluted NaOH solutions in a ratio of 1: ∼5.5 (molar ratio of "100 µL 2 g/L methyl red solutions: 40 µL 0.1 M NaOH solutions" and "100 µL 2 g/L bromocresol green solutions: 16 µL 0.1 M NaOH solutions"). The resulting color change and fluorescence intensity were apparent. The CIE coordinate values corresponded with the NaOH titration, and the emission colors changed sequentially between different colors. For methyl red, “red → orange → yellow → green” color variation can be observed, corresponding to the CIE values (0.49, 0.30), (0.51, 0.35), (0.38, 0.38), (0.24, 0.40), (0.21, 0.44), (0.20, 0.46), (0.20, 0.51), (0.21,0.53). As for bromocresol green, color changes occur in three areas, yellow → green → blue, the CIE values are listed as follows: (0.33, 0.51), (0.33, 0.51), (0.32, 0.50), (0.28, 0.48), (0.17, 0.32), (0.15, 0.13), (0.19, 0.07), (0.15, 0.06), (0.15, 0.06). (Table s3).

It is possible to adjust the fluorescence emission of WCDs by changing the indicator color through acid-base regulation. For methyl red, the absorption UV spectra of acidic and alkaline forms correspond to 420 nm and 490 nm, respectively. These fluorescent emissions have been adsorbed at certain wave ranges (see Fig s6). In the case of bromocresol green, the absorbance peaks of its three different forms can be observed at 416 nm (yellow), 624 nm (blue), 398 nm, and 620 nm (green) (see Fig s7). Due to the IFE [30], the WCDs emission light absorbed by bromocresol green displays three fluorescent colors that can be adjusted by NaOH titration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparing WCDs/Dye/PVA composite films with vibrant emissions
ce:section.ce:para: Efforts have also been made to prepare vibrant emissive films by leveraging the interesting emission of dyes with WCDs in solution [31–37]. In this regard, Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) was chosen as the prime candidate for preparing composite fluorescent films [38]. Doping WCDs with methyl red, bromocresol green, and methylene blue in PVA, can yield red, yellow, and blue colors WCDs/PVA composites. In Fig. 5c, we present optical micrographs of WCDs/PVA composite discs, measuring approximately 1.1 cm in diameter and 153–162 µm in thickness, as determined by a micrometer caliper. Following curing, the WCDs/PVA composites exhibit a range of colors attributable to the various added dyes. These PVA discs are transparent and emit uniform, multiple colors from blue to red (emission maxima at λ = 426, 529, and 603 nm) under corresponding excitation light, as depicted in Fig. 5b. The transmittance and wavelength relationship (T-λ) of these WCDs-dye PVA composite films were investigated. The red film showed the biggest transmittance region (∼70 %) above 600 nm, while the yellow film showed a significant increase in transmittance (Up to ∼65 %) between 475 and 550 nm. The blue film has the biggest transmittance (∼59 %) at about 472 nm, which is attributed to the blue light region (Fig. 5a). The CIE color coordinates for the fluorescence of three PVA composite films are (0.57, 0.32) for red film, (0.34, 0.51) for yellow film, and (0.18, 0.16) for blue film (as illustrated in Fig s8).

To sum up, the vibrant emissive films emitting positions and spectral profiles for the different chromatic emissions in the as-prepared composite films are close to that in the CD solution. This demonstrates that the inner filter effect of the dye has been maintained in these CD/PVA composite films, and the dye controlled the color of WCDs both in the solution and solid media.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Universality and advantage of multi-color emission composites formed by white carbon dots and dyes
ce:section.ce:para: The regulation of multi-color emission in CDs has been a subject of investigation owing to its fundamental chemical implications and the need to reveal the true nature of CDs [39–42]. The construction of white light-emitting diodes (LEDs) can be achieved through the combination of multi-color fluorescent CDs, which are excited by a single wavelength [43]. WCDs can be separated into distinct colors using suitable techniques such as polyamide chromatography [44]. The colors of CDs are usually influenced by various factors, including synthesis method, pH value [45], and concentration [46]. This manuscript presents a series of multi-color emission composites of WCDs and dyes based on the internal filtration effect [47]. WCDs can be utilized as raw materials to produce CDs of various colors, indicating that our method has universal applicability. The specific emission of carbon dots can be obtained by filtering dyes, which allows for the control of their fluorescence intensity and chromaticity (Fig. 3a-c, g-i). Spectral and CIE analysis demonstrated that the color of CDs-dye composites also exhibits pH responsiveness (WCDs + Methyl red, red → orange → yellow → green; WCDs + Bromocresol green, yellow → green → blue) (Fig. 4). This suggests that by combining carbon dots and dyes, multi-color CDs can be created in the future that react to stimuli such as light, electricity, water, and temperature based on the properties of the dyes.

In summary, a simple and precise method was established for producing a series of carbon dots composites with adjustable emissions across the visible spectrum. By combining white carbon dots with dyes, we achieved three primary colors of fluorescence through dyes’ inner filter effort. The inner filtration effect has been demonstrated by analyzing the relationship between ultraviolet absorption and fluorescence spectrum, excitation-emission matrix, and fluorescence lifetime, and ruling out dynamic and static quenching mechanisms. Additionally, the fluorescence of the colored carbon dots has also been modulated by color-changing dyes. Multicolor fluorescent composite films were prepared by dispersing carbon dots and dyes into polyvinyl alcohol films. In brief, this research provides a simple method for preparing multicolor carbon dots or their composite. This demonstrates the potential of our approach in developing various other multi-color fluorescent composites and provides a promising platform for developing advanced optical materials that can be applied to various fields such as biomedical imaging, anti-counterfeiting, and LEDs.

All the chemicals were commercially available at analytic grade and used as received. Fresh double distilled water was used throughout the study. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) observations were carried out on a JEOL JEM-2100 F microscope. The AFM images were captured with the Bruker Dimension ICON atomic force microscope. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained on a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS20 between 400 and 4000 cm−1 through a liquid cell. Raman spectra were carried out with Horiba LabRAM HR Evolution. The fluorescence data were determined on a Perkin-Elmer LS 45 luminescence spectrometer. For all luminescence measurements, excitation and emission slit widths of 10 nm were used. The wave number of excited light for a UV lamp is 365 nm. The PL(Photoluminescence) decay curves were measured by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh FSL1000) using a 60 W µs flash lamp as an excitation light source.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The synthesis and characterization of WCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent white carbon dots (labeled as WCDs) were prepared via a one-pot solvothermal process using tartaric acid (Tar) and o-phenylenediamine (oPD) as reactants [29]. The oPD (0.10 g) and Tar (0.10 g) were dissolved in 7.5 mL ethanol solution, then the solution was transferred into a 15 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180°C for 12 h ( Scheme 2). The solution was filtered with a 0.22 μm microporous membrane and then purified by dialysis bag with the MWCO of 1000 Da in ethanol solvent for 24 h. As shown in the FTIR spectrum of WCDs, there are four sharp peaks around 1000–1500 cm−1 attributed to the -NH2, -COOH, and -OH groups on the surface of WCDs. The acid-base stability of WCDs was investigated through UV spectroscopy, and it has shown a tiny spectral protrusion around 610 nm when the pH is less than 3.0, while the WCDs keep stable in the wide pH range (Fig s9).

ce:section.ce:section-title: General procedure for fluorescence and UV measurement
ce:section.ce:para: 0.01–0.10 mL WCDs solution and ethanol solution were taken into 1.0 cm quartz cuvette both in the fluorescence and UV measurement. The mixture was titrated by successive additions of methyl red, bromocresol green, and methylene blue working solution (2 g/L). Fluorescence titrations were done manually by using trace syringes, and the spectral signal was gained at excitation of 320–350 nm, and emission wavelength was set as 470, 575, and 690 nm. The experiments of the quenching mechanism were performed at three temperatures (283, 293, and 303 K). As for pH sensing properties of composites formed by WCDs and dyes, the NaOH and HCl dilute solutions (0.1 M) have been introduced in the ethanol system.

At ∼90 ℃, PVA is dissolved in water to form a 10–20 % transparent solution. Typically, we dissolved 2.0 g of PVA in 8.0 mL of water to create a 20 % PVA water solution. Then cool to 50 ℃, WCDs and dye solution were added proportionately to the PVA aqueous solution to form a mixture. The mixture was stirred to form a homogeneous mixture. The mixture was then put into the glass slide for 2–4 h at 50 ℃, then maintained at room temperature for curing, the red or blue emissive WCDs/PVA composite film was obtained. The time under 2 h may result in an unstable film with high volatile content. Exceeding 4 h doesn't significantly improve film quality but may impact experimental efficiency. For the yellow WCDs/PVA composites film, the stronger ammonia water was introduced to the methyl red solution, the indicator displayed the yellow color of the alkaline form. Additionally, the system of bromocresol green and WCDs can be adjusted by adding acid to obtain a yellow film. As a result, the red, yellow, and blue emissive films have been prepared successfully. The tabular form given is Tab s4, exhibiting the corresponding film composition.

I have made substantial contributions to the conception or design of the work; or the acquisition, analysis, or interpretation of data for the work; AND I have drafted the work or revised it critically for important intellectual content; AND I have approved the final version to be published; AND I agree to be accountable for all aspects of the work in ensuring that questions related to the accuracy or integrity of any part of the work are appropriately investigated and resolved. All persons who have made substantial contributions to the work reported in the manuscript, including those who provided editing and writing assistance but who are not authors, are named in the Acknowledgments section of the manuscript and have given their written permission to be named. If the manuscript does not include Acknowledgments, it is because the authors have not received substantial contributions from nonauthors.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declared that they have no conflicts of interest to this work. We declare that we do not have any commercial or associative interest that represents a conflict of interest in connection with the work submitted.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (22106051), the Jilin Provincial Department of Science and Technology (20240101155JC, YDZJ202401388ZYTS), the education department of Jilin Province (JJKH20240302KJ), and the State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals, Dalian University of Technology (KF2212).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.mtcomm.2024.110428.
